enrichment.short_description: Open-source, self-hosted multi-tenant PBX and telephony platform built on FreeSWITCH for calls, voicemail, and call centers.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: FusionPBX is an open-source communications platform built on top of the FreeSWITCH media server. It describes itself as a highly available, single- or domain-based multi-tenant PBX, carrier-grade switch, call center server, fax server, VoIP server, voicemail server, conference server, and voice application platform.

The system provides unlimited extensions, voicemail-to-email, music on hold, call parking, call queues, phone auto-provisioning, call recording with transcription and summaries, text-to-speech for voicemail greetings, and a WebSocket-driven admin dashboard, all managed through a self-hosted web GUI.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: FusionPBX targets small, medium, and large businesses and hosting providers that want enterprise-grade telephony features without commercial PBX licensing costs, provided they have the Linux and FreeSWITCH skills needed to run and secure their own deployment.

features.title: Multi-tenant PBX
features.description: Domain-based multi-tenant architecture lets a single install host many independent organizations or clients.

features.title: Call center and queues
features.description: Includes call queues, call parking, and call center tooling for support and sales teams.

features.title: Voicemail with transcription
features.description: Voicemail-to-email plus call recording transcription and summaries, with text-to-speech for greetings.

features.title: Real-time WebSocket dashboards
features.description: Live dashboards driven by WebSocket integration for monitoring calls and system status.

features.title: Built on FreeSWITCH
features.description: Runs on the FreeSWITCH media server, a scalable, multi-platform communications engine.

features.title: Security tooling
features.description: Advanced security features, including the Event Guard fraud/abuse protection module.

pros: Free and open source with no per-seat licensing
Feature-rich: call center, fax, conferencing, and voicemail in one stack
Built on the proven, actively developed FreeSWITCH core
Multi-tenant support suits hosting providers and MSPs
Active community and documentation
cons: Requires solid Linux and FreeSWITCH administration skills to deploy and secure
Configuration and call-routing logic has a steep learning curve
Official structured training and support are paid
Admin GUI is functional but dated compared to modern SaaS UIs

faqs.question: Is FusionPBX free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, the FusionPBX software itself is open source and free to download, install, and run on your own infrastructure.

faqs.question: What does FusionPBX run on?
faqs.answer: FusionPBX is a web-based management layer built on top of the FreeSWITCH media server.

faqs.question: Can FusionPBX host multiple tenants?
faqs.answer: Yes, it supports single-tenant or domain-based multi-tenant deployments, making it suitable for hosting providers.

faqs.question: Is paid support available?
faqs.answer: FusionPBX offers a separate paid membership program with training videos, documentation, and continuing education for teams that want structured support.

use_cases.title: Business phone system
use_cases.description: Self-hosted PBX with extensions, voicemail, and conferencing for a single organization.

use_cases.title: Managed telecom hosting
use_cases.description: Domain-based multi-tenancy lets a provider run many customer PBXs from one deployment.

use_cases.title: Call center deployment
use_cases.description: Queues, call recording, and reporting support inbound support and sales teams.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: FusionPBX is a free, open-source multi-tenant PBX built on FreeSWITCH for self-hosted calls, voicemail, and call centers.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source SIP server for building high-capacity VoIP, WebRTC, and messaging infrastructure at carrier scale.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Kamailio is an open-source SIP (Session Initiation Protocol) server designed to handle high-volume call signaling. The project traces its lineage to SIP Express Router (SER) and OpenSER, and today is released under the GPLv2+ license with development on GitHub.

Written in C for Unix/Linux, Kamailio can process thousands of call setups per second and supports VoIP, WebRTC, instant messaging, and presence, with asynchronous TCP/UDP/SCTP protocols, TLS, IPv4/IPv6, and WebSocket support. It integrates with databases such as MySQL and PostgreSQL, authentication backends like RADIUS and LDAP, and media servers such as Asterisk and FreeSWITCH.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Kamailio is aimed at telecom carriers, VoIP providers, and enterprises building unified communications infrastructure — for example, SIP load balancing, least-cost routing, SIP-to-PSTN gateways, and WebRTC signaling — who need a self-hosted, highly scriptable proxy rather than a managed service.

features.title: High-throughput SIP proxy
features.description: Handles thousands of call setups per second with asynchronous TCP/UDP/SCTP networking.

features.title: WebRTC and WebSocket support
features.description: Native WebSocket support enables browser-based WebRTC signaling alongside traditional SIP.

features.title: TLS and IPv4/IPv6
features.description: Secure transport and dual-stack networking for modern carrier deployments.

features.title: Database and directory integration
features.description: Connects to MySQL, PostgreSQL, Oracle, RADIUS, and LDAP for routing, auth, and provisioning data.

features.title: Presence and instant messaging
features.description: Built-in support for SIP presence and IM alongside voice/video signaling.

features.title: Interoperates with Asterisk and FreeSWITCH
features.description: Commonly deployed as a front-end proxy/load balancer in front of media servers.

pros: Free, GPL-licensed, with fully public source code
Battle-tested lineage dating back to the SER/OpenSER projects
Scales to thousands of calls per second on modest hardware
Highly modular and scriptable routing logic
Large community and extensive module ecosystem
cons: No built-in graphical admin interface
Configuration language has a steep learning curve
Requires solid SIP protocol knowledge to operate safely
Self-hosted deployment means you own scaling, security, and upgrades

faqs.question: Is Kamailio free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Kamailio is open source under the GPLv2+ license and free to deploy on your own servers.

faqs.question: Does Kamailio include a media server?
faqs.answer: No, Kamailio is a SIP signaling proxy/registrar; it's commonly paired with a media server such as Asterisk or FreeSWITCH.

faqs.question: Does Kamailio support WebRTC?
faqs.answer: Yes, via native WebSocket support for browser-based SIP/WebRTC signaling.

faqs.question: Who maintains Kamailio?
faqs.answer: It is developed by an open-source community and is available for review and contribution on GitHub.

use_cases.title: Carrier SIP proxy and registrar
use_cases.description: Route and load-balance high volumes of SIP traffic across a telecom network.

use_cases.title: WebRTC signaling gateway
use_cases.description: Bridge browser-based WebRTC clients into a SIP network via WebSocket support.

use_cases.title: SIP-to-PSTN gateway front-end
use_cases.description: Sit in front of Asterisk or FreeSWITCH to handle routing, load balancing, and security.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Kamailio is a free, open-source SIP server for carrier-grade VoIP, WebRTC, and messaging infrastructure, self-hosted on Linux.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source SIP proxy server for voice, video, messaging, and presence, built for carrier- and enterprise-scale telecom platforms.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: openSIPS is an open-source SIP proxy server used as a signaling layer for voice, video, instant messaging, presence, and other SIP-based communications. It offers over 120 modules covering SIP handling, backend integration, and routing logic, and is capable of handling tens of thousands of call attempts per second with millions of simultaneous calls.

Common deployment patterns include Class 4/5 softswitch platforms, SIP trunking, enterprise PBX front-ends, session border controllers, and SIP load balancing. The core software is open source; the project also offers a community SBC solution, donation-funded development, and commercial support through affiliated organizations.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: openSIPS is built for carriers, telecoms, and ITSPs (Internet Telephony Service Providers) that need a highly scalable, self-hosted SIP routing layer, as well as enterprises building their own VoIP infrastructure who want scriptable, module-based control over call routing.

features.title: 120+ modules
features.description: Modular architecture covers SIP routing, NAT traversal, accounting, presence, and backend integrations.

features.title: Massive throughput
features.description: Designed to handle tens of thousands of calls per second and millions of concurrent calls.

features.title: Custom scripted routing
features.description: Flexible routing logic through openSIPS's own scripting language.

features.title: SBC and load balancing
features.description: Can be deployed as a session border controller or SIP load balancer in front of media servers.

features.title: Class 4/5 softswitch support
features.description: Used to build wholesale (Class 4) and retail (Class 5) telecom platforms.

features.title: Commercial support option
features.description: Paid support and consulting are available alongside the free, open-source core.

pros: Free and open source with no licensing fees for the core platform
Proven at very large call volumes for carriers and ITSPs
Highly modular; only load the functionality you need
Commercial support and consulting available if needed
Long track record in production telecom deployments
cons: Requires deep SIP and telecom protocol expertise to configure safely
No polished built-in web GUI for the core proxy
Self-hosted operation means you manage scaling and security
Scripting-based routing has a learning curve for newcomers

faqs.question: Is openSIPS free?
faqs.answer: Yes, the core openSIPS SIP server is open source and free to self-host.

faqs.question: Can openSIPS act as a session border controller?
faqs.answer: Yes, openSIPS supports SBC use cases including a dedicated community SBC solution.

faqs.question: Is commercial support available?
faqs.answer: Yes, paid support and consulting are offered for organizations that need professional services on top of the free software.

faqs.question: What scale can openSIPS handle?
faqs.answer: It's designed for tens of thousands of calls per second and millions of simultaneous calls in carrier-grade deployments.

use_cases.title: Wholesale/retail softswitch
use_cases.description: Build Class 4 (wholesale) or Class 5 (retail) telecom platforms on open-source infrastructure.

use_cases.title: Enterprise SIP trunking
use_cases.description: Route and manage SIP trunks for enterprise voice infrastructure.

use_cases.title: SIP load balancing / SBC
use_cases.description: Front media servers with a scalable SIP proxy for load balancing and edge security.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: openSIPS is a free, open-source SIP proxy server for carrier-grade VoIP, SBC, and telecom routing, self-hosted on your own servers.

enrichment.short_description: Cloud-native, open-source SIP proxy, location server, and registrar built for Docker and Kubernetes VoIP infrastructure.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Routr is a cloud-native SIP server created by Fonoster, described on its site as a lightweight SIP proxy, location server, and registrar for building telephony infrastructure. It's designed to run on Docker and Kubernetes and can be controlled remotely via a command-line interface, a RESTful API, and a web GUI.
The project is open source and hosted on GitHub, where it has continued to receive releases and issue activity through late 2025, indicating ongoing maintenance.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Routr targets telephony carriers, communication service providers, and integrators or developers who want a container-native, API-driven SIP server rather than a traditional monolithic softswitch.

features.title: Docker/Kubernetes-native
features.description: Built to be deployed and orchestrated in containerized environments.

features.title: REST API control
features.description: Manage the SIP server programmatically through a RESTful API.

features.title: Web GUI and CLI
features.description: Configure and monitor the server through a web interface or command-line tooling.

features.title: SDKs for integration
features.description: SDKs are provided to help developers integrate Routr into VoIP applications.

features.title: Lightweight proxy/registrar
features.description: Focused feature set as a SIP proxy, location server, and registrar rather than a full PBX.

pros: Free and open source
Designed from the ground up for containers and Kubernetes
Multiple control surfaces: CLI, REST API, and web GUI
Actively maintained with releases and issue activity into late 2025
cons: Smaller community and ecosystem than Kamailio or openSIPS
Requires container/Kubernetes operational knowledge to run in production
Less enterprise tooling and third-party documentation than more established SIP servers

faqs.question: Is Routr free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, Routr is open source and free to self-host.

faqs.question: Does Routr require Kubernetes?
faqs.answer: It's designed to run well on Docker and Kubernetes, though it can be run in other Docker-based setups as well.

faqs.question: Can I manage Routr without the CLI?
faqs.answer: Yes, it also offers a RESTful API and a web GUI for remote management.

faqs.question: Who builds Routr?
faqs.answer: Routr is developed by Fonoster and maintained as an open-source project on GitHub.

use_cases.description: Handle SIP registration and routing for a carrier or CSP network.

enrichment.short_description: Real-time VoIP and RTC traffic monitoring platform with a free, open-source Community Edition for troubleshooting call quality.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: SIP3 monitors VoIP and RTC traffic in real time, giving teams a dashboard to troubleshoot live calls, search non-standard call flows, and review historical and trending call data. It is built from three main components — Captain (capture agent/data adapter), Twig (query/aggregation API), and Salto (the event-driven processing pipeline) — with an OpenAPI-based interface for integrations.

The SIP3 Community Edition components are published as open source on GitHub under the sip3io organization and can be self-hosted for free. SIP3 also offers commercial editions with vendor support for organizations that need it, though public pricing for those tiers is not published.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: SIP3 is aimed at VoIP service providers, contact centers, and enterprises with high call volumes who need real-time visibility into SIP/RTC traffic to reduce troubleshooting and support-ticket resolution time.

features.title: Real-time call analytics
features.description: Dashboard for monitoring and troubleshooting live VoIP/RTC calls as they happen.

features.title: OpenAPI integrations
features.description: OpenAPI-based interface for connecting SIP3 to third-party tools and infrastructure-as-code workflows.

features.title: Historical and trend analysis
features.description: Store and query historical call data to spot trends and recurring issues.

features.title: Custom search for call flows
features.description: Search and troubleshoot non-standard or unusual call flows quickly.

features.title: Event-driven processing pipeline
features.description: Captain, Twig, and Salto components form a scalable capture-and-query pipeline for high traffic volumes.

pros: Community Edition is free, open source, and self-hostable
Purpose-built for VoIP/RTC quality troubleshooting, not generic monitoring
High-performance pipeline designed for large traffic volumes
OpenAPI support fits infrastructure-as-code workflows
cons: Full commercial features and vendor support require a paid plan with pricing not publicly listed
Narrower focus than general-purpose network/APM monitoring tools
Requires deploying separate capture agents alongside the core platform

faqs.question: Is SIP3 free?
faqs.answer: The SIP3 Community Edition is open source and free to self-host; commercial editions with vendor support are also available but pricing isn't publicly listed.

faqs.question: What does SIP3 monitor?
faqs.answer: It monitors SIP signaling and RTC media traffic in real time for VoIP troubleshooting and quality analysis.

faqs.question: Can SIP3 integrate with other tools?
faqs.answer: Yes, it exposes an OpenAPI interface for third-party and infrastructure-as-code integrations.

use_cases.title: Live call troubleshooting
use_cases.description: Diagnose call quality and signaling issues in real time as they occur.

use_cases.title: Traffic trend analysis
use_cases.description: Review historical VoIP/RTC data to spot recurring problems.

features: Core capture and monitoring pipeline (Captain, Twig, Salto)

enrichment.seo_meta_description: SIP3 is a real-time VoIP and RTC monitoring platform with a free, open-source, self-hosted Community Edition for troubleshooting.

enrichment.short_description: 100% open-source SIP, VoIP, and RTC packet capture and monitoring platform built around the HEP capture protocol.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: HOMER, part of the SIPCAPTURE project, is a fully open-source SIP, VoIP, and RTC packet capture and monitoring system. It's a carrier-grade, modular capture framework used for analysis and monitoring, natively supporting major open-source voice platforms.

HOMER is built around the HEP/EEP capture protocol, which mirrors SIP/RTP/RTCP packets without altering the original traffic. Capture agents include HEPlify (written in Go) and CaptAgent (written in C), and native HEP support is built into OpenSIPS, Kamailio, Asterisk, and FreeSWITCH. HOMER can be deployed via Docker, with a web UI and HEP listeners on dedicated ports.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: HOMER is aimed at VoIP engineers, carriers, and platform operators who run OpenSIPS, Kamailio, Asterisk, or FreeSWITCH and need packet-level capture and monitoring for troubleshooting and analysis.

features.title: HEP/EEP capture protocol
features.description: Mirrors SIP, RTP, and RTCP traffic for analysis without modifying the original packets.

features.title: Native agent support
features.description: Native HEP integration is built into OpenSIPS, Kamailio, Asterisk, and FreeSWITCH.

features.title: HEPlify and CaptAgent
features.description: Dedicated capture agents (Go-based HEPlify, C-based CaptAgent) for near-zero-config or complex deployments.

features.title: Carrier-grade scale
features.description: Designed to capture, index, and analyze SIP/RTP/RTCP traffic at large scale.

features.title: Docker deployment
features.description: Deployable via Docker with a web interface for browsing captured traffic.

pros: Fully open source and free to self-host
Native integration with the most common open-source SIP platforms
Scales to carrier-grade traffic volumes
Docker-based deployment simplifies setup
cons: Requires deploying and configuring separate capture agents
Best suited to teams that already run OpenSIPS/Kamailio/Asterisk/FreeSWITCH
No official commercial support tier evident on the site

faqs.question: Is HOMER free?
faqs.answer: Yes, HOMER and the broader SIPCAPTURE stack are 100% open source and free to self-host.

faqs.question: What is the HEP protocol?
faqs.answer: HEP (Homer Encapsulation Protocol) mirrors packet data for capture and analysis without altering the original traffic.

faqs.question: Which platforms does HOMER integrate with?
faqs.answer: It has native support in OpenSIPS, Kamailio, Asterisk, and FreeSWITCH, plus standalone capture agents for other setups.

use_cases.title: SIP/VoIP troubleshooting
use_cases.description: Capture and inspect SIP signaling and RTP/RTCP media to diagnose call issues.

use_cases.title: Carrier traffic monitoring
use_cases.description: Monitor large-scale SIP traffic across a carrier network for QoS and security analysis.
use_cases.audience: Carriers and large VoIP platform operators

enrichment.seo_meta_description: SIPCAPTURE Homer is a free, open-source SIP/VoIP/RTC packet capture and monitoring platform built on the HEP protocol.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source, API-first platform for building carrier-grade, programmable IP communication infrastructure.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Wazo Platform is an open-source project for building carrier-grade, programmable IP communication infrastructure. It provides audio/video calling, chat, call center, and voicemail functionality through a microservices architecture written in Python with OpenAPI-documented interfaces.

The platform is built on proven open-source telecom components, including Asterisk, Kamailio, RTPEngine, RabbitMQ, PostgreSQL, and Nginx, and exposes WebRTC, REST APIs, WebSockets, and webhooks for mobile and web integration. It's released under the GPLv3 license and can be deployed on bare metal, virtual machines, or containers.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Wazo is aimed at developers and organizations that want to build customizable, programmable telecom products without vendor lock-in — using Wazo's APIs and open-source stack instead of a proprietary UCaaS platform.

features.title: API-first design
features.description: REST APIs, WebSockets, and webhooks with OpenAPI specifications for building custom communication apps.

features.title: WebRTC calling
features.description: Native WebRTC support for browser and mobile audio/video calling.

features.title: Built on proven OSS telecom stack
features.description: Combines Asterisk, Kamailio, and RTPEngine with RabbitMQ and PostgreSQL under the hood.

features.title: Call center and voicemail
features.description: Includes call center features and voicemail alongside core calling.

features.title: Flexible deployment
features.description: Runs on bare metal, VMs, or containers, giving operators control over infrastructure.

pros: Fully open source under GPLv3, no licensing fees
API-first architecture makes it easy to build custom integrations
Built on proven, well-established open-source telecom components
Deployment flexibility across bare metal, VM, or container environments
cons: Requires Linux/Debian systems administration skills to self-host
Microservices architecture adds operational complexity versus a single-binary PBX
Smaller ecosystem and community than Asterisk alone

faqs.question: Is Wazo free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Wazo Platform is open source under the GPLv3 license and free to self-host.

faqs.question: What is Wazo built on?
faqs.answer: It's built on established open-source components including Asterisk, Kamailio, RTPEngine, RabbitMQ, PostgreSQL, and Nginx.

faqs.question: Does Wazo support WebRTC?
faqs.answer: Yes, WebRTC is supported natively for browser and mobile calling.

faqs.question: How is Wazo deployed?
faqs.answer: It can run on bare metal, virtual machines, or containers, depending on your infrastructure.

use_cases.title: Programmable business phone system

use_cases.title: Custom UCaaS product
use_cases.description: Use Wazo's APIs and OSS stack as the foundation for a branded communications product.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source SIP Session Border Controller, softswitch, and real-time VoIP billing system for carrier interconnection.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Yeti-Switch is an open-source SIP Session Border Controller, softswitch, and real-time VoIP billing system built on the SEMS SIP/RTP stack. It unifies carrier interconnection, dynamic routing (least-cost routing, load balancing, capacity management), and real-time billing for wholesale and retail telecom services in a single platform.

The platform includes a number portability database, STIR/SHAKEN call authentication support, Microsoft Teams Direct Routing compatibility, a REST API for automation, and a customer-facing web portal, with installation docs, a live demo, and community forum support.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Yeti-Switch targets telecom carriers and VoIP providers running Class 4 softswitch operations — both wholesale transit and retail service providers — who need combined routing, SBC, and billing functionality without stitching together separate commercial products.

features.title: SBC and softswitch in one
features.description: Combines session border control with Class 4 softswitch routing on the SEMS SIP/RTP stack.

features.title: Real-time billing
features.description: Integrated wholesale and retail billing tied directly to call routing and CDRs.

features.title: Dynamic routing
features.description: Least-cost routing, load balancing, and capacity management across carrier trunks.

features.title: STIR/SHAKEN authentication
features.description: Supports STIR/SHAKEN for call authentication and caller ID verification.

features.title: Microsoft Teams Direct Routing
features.description: Compatible with Teams Direct Routing for enterprise voice integrations.

features.title: REST API and customer portal
features.description: Automate operations via REST API and give customers self-service access through a web portal.

pros: Combines SBC, softswitch, and billing in one open-source stack
STIR/SHAKEN and Microsoft Teams Direct Routing support built in
REST API enables automation of routing and provisioning
Includes a customer-facing self-service portal
cons: Steep learning curve for teams without carrier/telecom background
Requires self-hosted infrastructure and SEMS stack expertise
Documentation is technical and aimed at telecom engineers

faqs.question: Is Yeti-Switch free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it's open source and free to self-host; no licensing fee is listed on the official site.

faqs.question: Does Yeti-Switch include billing?
faqs.answer: Yes, it includes integrated real-time billing for wholesale and retail VoIP services.

faqs.question: Does it support STIR/SHAKEN?
faqs.answer: Yes, STIR/SHAKEN call authentication is supported for caller ID verification and robocall mitigation.

faqs.question: Can I try Yeti-Switch before deploying?
faqs.answer: Yes, the official site offers a live demo and installation documentation.

use_cases.title: Retail VoIP service billing

enrichment.short_description: Open-source, self-hosted ActivityPub microblogging server forked from Pleroma, compatible with Mastodon apps.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Akkoma is a decentralized, self-hostable social networking server and part of the Fediverse — a network of independently operated servers that interoperate through the ActivityPub protocol. It's a specialized fork of Pleroma, developed with faster release cycles and feature choices that lean closer to Misskey.

Akkoma supports posting statuses with unlimited media attachments, following users on any ActivityPub-compatible service, custom emoji reactions, Misskey-Flavoured Markdown with animations, unlimited post editing, quote posts, and polls. It is compatible with existing Mastodon client apps and frontends, and its own site claims meaningfully faster performance than Pleroma for common operations.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Akkoma is for individuals, communities, and organizations that want to run their own social media server rather than relying on a centralized platform — particularly Pleroma admins who want a faster-moving fork with richer post formatting and reaction features.

features.title: ActivityPub federation
features.description: Interoperates with the wider Fediverse, including Mastodon, through the ActivityPub protocol.

features.title: Mastodon client compatibility
features.description: Works with existing Mastodon apps and frontends for posting and browsing.

features.title: Custom emoji reactions
features.description: React to posts with custom emoji beyond a simple like/favorite.

features.title: Rich post formatting
features.description: Misskey-Flavoured Markdown with animation support for expressive posts.

features.title: Unlimited edits and quote posts
features.description: Edit published posts without limit and quote other posts, plus create polls.

features.title: Faster than Pleroma
features.description: The project reports meaningfully faster performance than Pleroma for common operations.

pros: Free and open source
Full control over your own social media server via self-hosting
Compatible with the broader Mastodon/Fediverse app ecosystem
More frequent releases and richer formatting than upstream Pleroma
No centralized authority controlling your account or data
cons: Requires server administration skills to self-host and moderate
Smaller install base and plugin/theme ecosystem than Mastodon
Relies on community/donation-based development and support

faqs.question: Is Akkoma free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Akkoma is open source and free to self-host.

faqs.question: Is Akkoma the same as Mastodon?
faqs.answer: No, it's a separate ActivityPub server forked from Pleroma, but it works with Mastodon-compatible client apps.

faqs.question: Can Akkoma communicate with Mastodon users?
faqs.answer: Yes, because it implements ActivityPub, Akkoma servers federate with Mastodon and other Fediverse platforms.

faqs.question: Do I need to self-host Akkoma?
faqs.answer: Yes, Akkoma is self-hosted software; you (or a host you choose) run the server.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source, self-hosted Q&A platform under the Apache Software Foundation for building forums and knowledge bases.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Apache Answer is a free, open-source Q&A platform for teams of any scale, usable as a community forum, help center, or knowledge management system. It provides community-driven question-and-answer functionality with voting and commenting, an intuitive tagging system for organizing and searching content, and reputation points and badges to reward participation.
The project is licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0, supports self-hosting on your own infrastructure, and offers plugin support for extending functionality and connecting to other tools.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Apache Answer suits organizations of any size that want to build an internal knowledge base, a public community forum, or a customer support Q&A site, and prefer a self-managed, customizable platform over a hosted third-party forum service.

features.title: Voting and commenting Q&A
features.description: Community members ask questions, post answers, vote, and comment.

features.title: Tagging and search
features.description: Intuitive tagging system for organizing and finding content.

features.title: Plugin extensibility
features.description: Plugin support extends functionality and connects Answer to other tools.

features.title: Reputation and badges
features.description: Gamification through reputation points and badges rewards active, helpful users.

features.title: Apache-licensed open source
features.description: Free to use and modify under the Apache License 2.0.

pros: Free, Apache 2.0-licensed open source software
Purpose-built Q&A format suits knowledge bases and support forums
Plugin system allows extending core functionality
Built-in gamification (reputation, badges) encourages participation
Backed by the Apache Software Foundation's governance model
cons: Newer project with a smaller plugin/theme ecosystem than long-established forum software
Requires self-hosting infrastructure; no managed/cloud hosting mentioned by the project itself
Best suited to structured Q&A rather than free-form discussion threads

faqs.question: Is Apache Answer free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it's open source and free to use under the Apache License 2.0.

faqs.question: Is Apache Answer self-hosted?
faqs.answer: Yes, you deploy and run it on your own infrastructure.

faqs.question: What's it used for?
faqs.answer: It's used to build community forums, help centers, and internal knowledge bases with a voting-driven Q&A format.

faqs.question: Can I extend Apache Answer's functionality?
faqs.answer: Yes, it supports plugins to add features and integrate with other tools.

use_cases.title: Internal knowledge base
use_cases.description: Let employees ask and answer questions in a searchable, tagged Q&A format.
use_cases.audience: Internal IT and knowledge management teams

use_cases.title: Public community forum
use_cases.description: Run a public Q&A community around a product, project, or topic.

use_cases.title: Customer support Q&A
use_cases.description: Offer a self-hosted support forum where customers and staff can answer questions.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Apache Answer is a free, open-source, self-hosted Q&A platform for building community forums and knowledge bases.
